text: The structural changes that Cu undergoes under electrochemical conditions have been thoroughly studied and it has been proposed that they are closely related to the large reactivity exhibited by Cu during the CO2 reduction reaction. In this respect, roughening is a surface process which leads to Cu activation through the formation of a high number of atomic vacancies and adatoms. However, to date it has not been possible to determine the atomic structure at the surface of Cu upon adsorption of species and other reactants of relevance for the CO2 Electroreduction Reaction. Within this work, we are exploring the Cu chemical space upon adsorption of CO and H2 within electrochemical conditions using a mixed methodology approach that combines grand canonical Density Functional Theory and global optimization techniques in order to yield a potential-dependent grand canonical ensemble representation of the Cu surface upon variable CO and H2 coverages. This ensemble lies in a regime of intermediate coverages and mixed-species coverages that favours moderate elevated Cu surface species, which are however thermodynamically not favored but kinetically reachable in the evolution path of the system. In order to study this path, a quasi-kinetic Monte Carlo method has been applied to a simulated cathodic scan. This allows obtaining the evolution path in coverage space of the system over time and accessing the metastable structures along this trajectory. The metastable states are found at elevated Cu* species and Cu*CO. A detailed description of the Cu*CO species through the analysis of their chemical bonding is provided. By using molecular dynamics simulations and free energy calculations, the possible diffusion/dimerization mechanisms of the Cu*CO species along the surface are unveiled.
